lines.text: The bell hasn't finished ringing before Ms. Alvarez is already writing on the board.
Alright. Settle in — this one's worth talking about.
Team showcase. Two weeks out. Twenty-five percent of your grade this quarter.
A ripple of groans moves through the room.
Twenty-five percent? Ms. Alvarez, my heart cannot handle this kind of commitment this early in the year.
Your heart will survive, Marcus.
Groups of three. I'm assigning them — no, that includes you not picking your friends.
Theo Osei. Marcus Brody. Priya Shah. You're a team.
Honestly? I was kind of hoping for at least one total stranger. Keeps things professional.
Oh, we're gonna get along just fine.
It says the rubric's on the back. Did anyone else notice that.
lines.action: already reading the sheet a second time

lines.text: Priya read the syllabus. The rest of you, catch up.
Before you scatter — I want each team to divide roles today. Who's doing what. I'm not picking for you this time.
Library study room. First real planning session.
Okay so, basically — my sister did this exact project last year. Same category, everything. Won a ribbon at regionals.
...Okay?
So we could just use hers. As like — a starting point. Nobody's gonna know, it's not like Ms. Alvarez was at regionals.
You mean copy it.
I mean — adapt it. There's a difference.
lines.emotion: grin that doesn't quite reach his eyes

lines.action: taps a rhythm on the table, says nothing yet

lines.text: Come on, it'd save us so much time. We could actually be done early for once.

lines.action: eyes flick to the player, waiting

lines.text: No sister's ribbon to lean on. Just three kids and a blank poster board.
Okay so if we're doing this the hard way, what are we even doing.
I had an idea. It's probably stupid.
No such thing. Hit me.
Okay, so what if the base actually rotated — not just a diagram. It'd show the process instead of just labeling it.
Wait — like it spins? That's actually kind of sick.
You think so?
I mean, harder to build. But yeah. That's ours, at least.
Honestly? Kind of relieved. I did not want to explain to my mom why I'm nervous about a volcano.
It's not even a volcano this time, Theo.
I know. Force of habit.
Two weeks. It's tight. But it's ours.
The file's already open. Marcus's sister's old slides — swapped title, new font.
See, look — we just swap the color scheme, change the school name, boom. Basically new.

lines.action: glances at the original file tab still open in the corner, stomach tightening

lines.text: This is so much faster than I thought it'd be.
It is fast.
See? Told you nobody's gonna know.
Two days to deadline. Marcus's part of the model — still just cardboard and good intentions.
It says last edited four days ago. Four days, Marcus.
lines.action: refreshing the shared folder for the third time in a minute

lines.text: It's fine, it's fine, I got it, I just— I got it.
You said that on Tuesday.

lines.action: watches, taps own rhythm on the desk, trying not to say something

lines.text: We are out of time.
Hallway outside the library. Everyone else already gone.
Can I ask you something.
...Yeah, sure.
This is basically his sister's project with a new title slide. Doesn't that feel wrong to you?
I keep telling myself it's fine because it's fast, and it's good, and everyone seems okay with it. But I checked the folder again last night and I couldn't — I couldn't stop thinking about it.
Hallway by the lockers, after everyone else has cleared out.
Marcus. Seriously — what's going on. You're not usually like this.
...Nothing.
Come on. It's not nothing. Priya's stressed, I'm stressed, and you keep saying 'I got it' about a part that's very much not gotten.
...Yeah. Things have been kind of a lot at home. I didn't— I didn't know how to say that without it turning into a whole thing.
It wouldn't have. I mean — it's not, right now.
Yeah. Guess not.
You should've just said something.
I know. I'm bad at that part.
Better at the jokes part.
Noted. So — what do you actually need?
Honestly? Just — help. Like, actual help. Not you guys being mad at me quietly.
We can do that.
Kitchen table, past midnight. Glue, cardboard, and a piece that isn't even the player's to build.
Nobody asked me to do this. Nobody's gonna know I did it, either.
It's fine. I'm fine. Definitely not gonna think about this for the rest of my life.

lines.action: finishes the final piece, sets it down

lines.text: There. Done. Marcus never even has to know anything was wrong.

lines.action: stares at the finished piece, no satisfaction on his face

lines.text: The project is finished. Nobody says thank you. Nobody knows there's anything to thank.
Ms. Alvarez's classroom, after the last bell.
Ms. Alvarez? Can I talk to you about something.
Of course.
Marcus hasn't done his part of the project. We're two days out and Priya and I have basically everything else done.
I appreciate you telling me the truth instead of letting it slide, Theo.
lines.action: taps two fingers on the desk

lines.text: Send Marcus in on your way out.
Later.
Marcus. Sit.

lines.action: sits, doesn't look at the player on his way out

lines.text: Finish your part. Then we'll talk about the part you skipped.
Marcus finishes it that night. He doesn't quite look at Theo the next day.
Priya's living room, that evening. The old slides are open — about to be deleted.
Marcus. We need to talk about the project.
...Okay?
We're not using your sister's project. Not like this.
Are you serious? We're two days—
We know. But it doesn't feel right. It hasn't, for a while.
...Yeah. Honestly? Me neither. I just didn't want to be the one to say it first.
Wait — really?
It felt kind of gross, ngl. Like wearing someone else's jacket to a game you didn't even play in.
So. Starting over. Tonight.
Okay. Okay — what've we got.
I had an idea a while back. Let me show you.
They work past dinner. It's slower, and harder, and entirely theirs.
Library study room. The project moves fast now. Almost too fast.
Okay, next slide, we just need the process labels—
...Okay.
lines.action: types what she's told, doesn't offer more

lines.text: You good, Priya? You've been kinda quiet.
It's fine.
lines.emotion: tone that makes clear it isn't

lines.action: notices, says nothing, taps a rhythm on his own desk

lines.text: The project gets finished. Priya stops sitting next to the player at lunch.
Library. Priya packs her bag a little too neatly, a little too fast.
Wait, where's she going—
It's your project. I think you two should finish deciding what to do with it.
...Okay? Theo, what's she talking about.
Whatever, we've got a deadline.
lines.action: shrugs, goes back to the laptop

lines.text: By the next day, word's gotten around: Theo wouldn't own the decision. Priya's polite in meetings now. Nothing else.
Presentation day. The rotating base actually works. Mostly.
Good work, all three of you.
Now — Q&A. Who contributed what?

lines.action: goes quiet, glances at the player, unsure

lines.text: Midway through the presentation, Ms. Alvarez tilts her head at the display board.
This looks a lot like a project from the regional fair a couple years back. Where did the idea come from?
The room goes very quiet. Priya's pen stops moving.
Hallway, after the bell.
Hey. Thanks for saying that in there.
It was true.
Still. Nobody's ever really —
Anyway. Thanks.
That's what teams are for. Allegedly.
Later, at lunch.
Okay but did you guys actually finish it or was Marcus still 'working on his part' in the metaphysical sense.
Hallway, after the bell.
I did the base. Eventually.
Later, at lunch.
Okay but did you guys actually finish it or was Marcus still 'working on his part' in the metaphysical sense.
Classroom, after the bell. Ms. Alvarez is writing something in green pen.
A zero teaches you to hide better next time. A redo teaches you something else. I'd rather teach you the second thing.
...Thank you.
Don't thank me. Rebuild it. Properly, this time.
You have until Friday.
We can do that.
lines.emotion: relieved for the first time in days

lines.text: Yeah. We got it. For real this time.
Later, at lunch.
Okay but did you guys actually finish it or was Marcus still 'working on his part' in the metaphysical sense.
Classroom, after the bell. The grade's already in — green ink, high marks.

lines.action: won't meet the player's eyes, gathering her things quickly

lines.text: Priya—
It's fine.
lines.action: leaves before he can say anything else

lines.text: The grade doesn't feel like a grade should.
Later, at lunch.
Okay but did you guys actually finish it or was Marcus still 'working on his part' in the metaphysical sense.

options.text: Team leader - I'll help keep us on track.
Researcher - I'll dig into the material.
Support role - I'll help wherever's needed.

a.text: Oh, look at you. Mr. Responsibility.
Good. Someone should keep the schedule honest.

b.text: That's — actually really helpful. I was worried I'd have to do that alone.

c.text: Solid. Flexible. I respect a man with no ego.
That's not really a role, Theo.
But — okay. We'll figure it out.

options.text: "That's basically cheating. Let's come up with our own idea."
"I mean... let's at least look at it for inspiration."
"Maybe we should ask Ms. Alvarez what's actually allowed first."

a.text: Thank you.
Fine, fine — worth a shot.
a.emotion: shrugging it off, not really upset yet

b.text: See, that's all I'm saying. Just a look.

c.text: Nah, nah, we don't need to make it a whole thing—

options.text: Say something now, even if it's awkward.
Push the feeling down and keep going - it's working, after all.

a.text: Hey — can we, um. Can we actually talk about this for a second?
Talk about what, it's fine—

a.action: quiet, grateful glance at the player

b.action: says nothing, taps a rhythm on the desk, keeps scrolling

b.text: It's fine. It's working. Everyone's happy. Except the thing in my stomach that won't shut up.

options.text: Pull Marcus aside and ask what's actually going on.
Quietly do his part yourself so it gets done.
Tell Ms. Alvarez he's not pulling his weight.

a.text: Marcus. Hey — can we talk. Actually talk.
...About what.

b.text: It's fine. I'll just — I'll handle it.
Yeah. Honestly, it's easier this way.

c.text: I think we need to tell Ms. Alvarez Marcus isn't doing his part.
That feels like a big step.
Maybe. But we're out of time.

options.text: "You're right. Let's tell Marcus and start over, honestly."
"We've come too far now - let's just keep this between us."
"It was Marcus's idea, not mine - go talk to him."

a.text: Okay. Okay — thank you.
We'll figure it out. Together.

options.text: Speak up and credit the whole team, including how Marcus turned things around.
Let Marcus answer for himself and stay out of it.

a.text: Priya designed the mechanism, I handled research and build, and Marcus — Marcus had a rough patch, but he came through when it counted. This wouldn't be finished without all three of us.
...Thanks.
That's a good answer, Theo.

b.text: I, uh — I did the base assembly. Eventually.

b.action: small, unreadable look at the player

options.text: Admit it was based too closely on an old project, and apologize.
Insist it's completely original.

a.text: ...It was based on an old project. My teammate's sister's, actually. We should've built our own from the start. I'm sorry.
Thank you for telling me the truth, Theo.
a.action: taps two fingers on the desk

b.text: No, it's — it's completely original. We just, um, we all like similar shapes I guess.

options.text: "Honestly? Rough start, but we pulled together in the end."
"It went fine." (leave it there)

a.text: Wait, deadass? Okay you have to tell me everything, lowkey I live for team drama.

options.text: "Fine, but I keep wondering if I should've said more in there."
"It worked out. That's all that matters."

a.text: Wait— you good? You've got the face. The face you get.
Yeah. I'm good. Just — thinking.

b.text: ...Okay, mysterious. I respect it. Kind of.

options.text: "Terrifying, honestly. But I'm glad I owned up to it."
"It's fine now, no big deal." (downplay it)

a.text: Wait, deadass? That's lowkey brave. I would've just melted into the floor.
I almost did.

b.emotion: not quite buying it, but drops it

options.text: Admit quietly that you're worried the truth will still come out.
Insist everything's fine, to Sam and to yourself.

a.text: ...Honestly? I'm kind of waiting for the other shoe to drop.
Wait— what's that supposed to mean?
Nothing. Forget it.

b.text: Everything's fine. We got a great grade.
...Okay? Why do you sound like you're trying to convince yourself.

lines.text: For what it's worth — thanks for actually asking instead of just getting mad. Nobody's really done that before.
You just had to say something eventually. I just got there first.
...We're good, right? Me and you?
Yeah. We're good.
Ngl, starting over was the right call. Wouldn't have felt right walking in there with my sister's ribbon on our backs.
It's better this way. Slower. But better.
It got done. Nobody got in trouble. So why does it still feel unfinished.

lines.action: watches Marcus walk off, doesn't call after him

lines.text: I know what was actually going on with Marcus. I just didn't say it out loud when it counted.
That's on me. A little.
We started over the honest way. I just wish I'd said that part out loud, in front of everyone.
You did fine, Theo.
Two weeks ago this would've been the whole project, done. Now it's just the start again.
Somehow that feels better.
I'm glad you told the truth. Even if it took this long to get here.
...Yeah. Me too.
The grade's in. Nobody caught it.
So why does it feel like I'm still holding my breath.
Priya hasn't really talked to me since.
I keep telling myself that'll pass. I don't totally believe it.

voiceNotes: Theo (player) leans on 'honestly' right before he says something he's nervous about, and turns statements into questions when unsure — his choice lines should read as slightly self-deprecating jokes covering real discomfort ('Honestly? I was hoping for at least one stranger.'). Marcus never says 'I lied' or 'I'm struggling' directly — he minimizes with 'basically' and 'it's fine, it's fine,' and only drops the performer's cadence in scene-5-direct-talk and scene-5-redo-honest, where his monosyllabic '...Yeah' is the tell that something real is surfacing. Priya rarely raises her voice — her hurt shows as clipped one-liners ('It's fine.' / 'Right. Of course.') rather than volume; give her the four-count pen tap as a physical beat before her quieter lines. Ms. Alvarez should almost never use exclamation points; her authority comes from precision and the pause before she speaks, and she uses full names ('Theo,' 'Marcus.') only when something matters. Sam's dialogue is intentionally consistent across all four endings (same opening question) so the *player's* answer is what changes — Sam's follow-up should always sound like genuine curiosity that sometimes brushes past the emotional weight, then catches it a beat late ('Wait— you good? You've got the face.').

alternatives: Cool, cool, cool. Totally normal amount of nerves happening right now.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps the turned-statement-into-question tic if the writer wants a lighter opening beat instead of the 'stranger' joke.

alternatives: That's her project. With our names on it.
So... we're just going to call that ours?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Priya's line can land more clipped/direct if the scene needs a harder tonal turn earlier.

alternatives: I know. I'm better at deflecting than at asking for help.
Yeah. Turns out 'it's fine' isn't actually a personality trait.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Offers a slightly more self-aware version of Marcus's admission if the performance needs more warmth going into the resolution.
The word 'Congratulations' said flat could land harder as an ending gut-punch if the scene wants more overt bitterness from Priya instead of pure withdrawal.
